description: Apollo 13 Flight Journal - Day 4, part 5: Checking Odyssey

tokens: Almost five days into the mission, the crew of the stricken Apollo 13 spacecraft has been relatively busy for the past 15 hours or so.

They have performed the PC+2 burn to cut 10 hours off their return time, powered down the LM, fashioned an adapter for the carbon dioxide scrubbers and received instructions on how to configure the Command ModuleOdysseyinto a state from which a startup may be enacted once the time comes for reentry into Earth's atmosphere.
For now, the crew is simply hanging on, saving water and electricity and hoping that nothing else will fail on board their spacecraft.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This onboard film capture shows Jack Swigert at the Commander's station in theAquarius.
Above him is the docking window.
The somewhat messy-looking white arrangement behind him towards the back of the cabin is the LM storage shelf.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: That completes that procedure, and the next thing I've got for you is a procedure for going back into the Command Module and powering up the main buses temporarily using the Bus Tie switches.

We want to do this for two reasons: first of all, we want it absolutely verified that there are no loads on the main buses, that we've got: everything off and that the buses look good; and the second thing we want to do is to power the bus - the main buses, with the Bus Tie motor switches, and then depower them by pulling the circuit breakers, leaving the Main Bus Tie switches in the On position, just to assure that they'll be there when we need them, whether the batteries get cold or not.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Did you copy my rationale for the main bus powerup?
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: You want to remove - You want to power up both buses, with the Bus Tie motor switches; first of all, to see that there are no loads on the buses, and second, and then depower the buses by pulling the circuit breakers to insure that the motor switches don't - that the Bus Ties stay on so that they're there when we need them.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Mission Control's plan is to get some battery power onto the Command Module DC buses.
This is done to make sure that everything is powered down, and will not disturb the system once they begin to power up again.
Another concern are the motor switches, which are remote electric switches controlled with circuit breakers and On/Off switches on the Main Display Console.
There is some concern that the mechanical switches might be freezing due to the dropping temperatures.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Battery power is stored in three entry and postlanding batteries in the Command Module.
There are two Battery Buses, and each can be powered by connecting the batteries to them.
The worrisome motor switches are used to connect the Battery Buses into the Main DC Buses.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Are you ready to copy the procedure?
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: As the first step, I have one change to the basic configuration that we gave you, and it's two more circuit breakers that we want open.
On panel 225 - Are you ready to write that down?
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: On panel 225, we want the Rendezvous Transponder Flight Bus circuit breaker open, and the S-Band FM Transmitter/DSE Flight Bus circuit breaker open.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: TV Rendezvous Transponder Flight Bus, open; TV S-Band Transmitter/DSE Flight Bus open on panel 225.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: They do not need the transponder nor the data transmitter unit powered, hence the circuit breakers are pulled.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: A complete checklist of the procedure read up to the crew now is found in the Flight Operations Report and reproduced below:
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And now we'll go into the main bus power up procedure.
And the first step there, is on panel 5.
We want the Bat Charger Bat A Charge circuit breaker closed, and the Bat Charger Bat B Charge circuit breaker closed.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: That's Bat Charger Bat A Charge, Bat Charger Bat B Charge, both closed on panel 5.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: These circuit breakers apply power to the Battery Buses A and B, allowing their voltages to be checked from the displays on the MDC, as well as enabling the battery charger to charge these batteries, when used.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We need them closed to power the switches.
Next on panel 5, we want the EPS Sensor Signal Main A and Main B circuit breakers closed.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Panel 5, CB EPS sensor's signals Main A and Main B closed.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: These circuit breakers enable the voltage on the Main Buses to be displayed on the onboard instrumentation as well as the telemetry.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We need them closed so that you can read out your volts and amps.
Next step, on panel 250, we want circuit breaker Bat A Power Entry and Postlanding and Bat B Power Entry and Postlanding, closed.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Okay on panel 250, CB Bat A Power, Entry and Postlanding, closed; Bat B Power Entry and Postlanding, closed.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: These circuit breakers connect Battery A and Battery B to their respective battery buses.
These will be required to get the battery power into the Main Buses later.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And at that point, we'd like you to check the Bat bus voltages on both Bat buses to make sure we don't have any shorts.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Read the Bat bus voltages on Bat Bus A and Bat Bus B.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: The next step is for Jack to use the rotating switch on Panel 3 to select the two Battery Buses, A and B, for readout on the voltage gauge.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The next step on panel 275: circuit breaker Main A, Bat Bus A, closed, and circuit breaker Main B, Bat Bus B, closed.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: On 275, circuit breakers Main A, Bat Bus A, closed; Main B, Bat Bus B, closed.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: The two breakers closed in this step apply Battery Bus power to the DC Main Buses A and B, through the 80 amp breakers.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The next step on panel 5: we want the Main Bus Tie A/C switch to the Bat A/C position, and verify proper voltage on Main Bus A, and read the amps on Bat A to make sure we don't have a short.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: This step begins the primary part of the whole procedure, which is to ensure that the battery bus tie motor switches are functioning.
By using the Main Bus Tie switch on the console, they operate the motor switch that connects the Battery Bus to the Main Bus.
Afterwards, Jack will check the voltages and the battery amperes from the Panel 3 displays he used earlier to check the Battery Bus voltage.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We're in bad comm again; let's wait a minute.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Place Main Bus Tie A/C, on, read Main Bus A current and voltage.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: The next step will be the Main Bus Tie B/C switch to the On position and read Main B voltage and battery B amperage as above.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Okay, Main Bus Tie B/C on; read Main B voltage and current.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: This step is similar to the previous one, but this time they are connecting Battery Bus B to the DC Main Bus B and checking that the motor switch works on that part of the power distribution system as well.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Then to reconfigure from this configuration, Jack, we want you to leave the Main Bus Tie switches in the On position and pull the circuit breakers out in reverse order that we had you close them.
Over; and do you want me to read them up individually?
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: The last step leaves the motor switches engaged, in the hope of preventing any future troubles with them should the low temperature cause them to get stuck in either open or closed position.
They can use the circuit breakers to continue to provide protection to the EPS and to serve as the On/Off switches for the time.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: No, let me see if I've got it here.
I go down to panel 275, open CB Main A, Bat Bus A and CB Main B, Bat Bus B.

Then I go down to 250, and pull CB Bat A Power Entry/Postlanding and Bat B Power Entry/Postlanding and then up on panel 5, pull EPS Sensor Signal Main A, Sensor Signal Main B, Battery Charger Bat B Charge, Battery Charger Bat A Charge.
And then close - I don't know whether you want me to close the Rendezvous Radar Transponder Flight Bus and S-Band Transmitter/DSE Flight Bus.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: We don't want you to close those last two.
Those are changes to your basic configuration, and we want to leave them open for now.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Was the rest of the readback okay?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Readback was 100 per cent, and we'll wait to hear from you.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: And just for confirmation, I went through the switch list you gave me.
We are in exactly that configuration with one exception, and that is over on panel 382.
I have not, the a - H2O Accumulator is in the Off position so that if we need any more water, we can get it.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: On panel 382, you've left the H2O Accumulator valves in the Off position, and we concur.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: And there's one other thing that I don't know whether you're aware of.
We have no lithium hydroxide canisters in panel two fif - in - canisters now.
So when we get ready to power up, you'll have to remind us when you want us to add some.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: That's correct, and I'll add that to our basic checklist so that we won't forget it.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Real fine, Joe, and I'm on my way back into the Command Module.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Jack heads up the tunnel into the Command Module.
In its unpowered stage, it was mostly dark, with the sunlight coming through the windows providing illumination only when the positioning was right to do so.
The picture shows the sizeable docking hatch to the left and the shiny shape of the docking probe to the right, stowed in the Command Module to be out of the way of the crew in the LM.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And, Joe, how do you read Aquarius?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I'm back on the line now, but Jack really [Garble], headed back to the upstairs bedroom.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: We recommend that you push your Master Alarm circuit breaker in so you'll have audio and visual on that, and, also, we medical types are getting to feel left out down here, and we'd like you to give us BIOMED [garble] position, if you're plugged in for it, and we'll see whether it affects the voice or not.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: They want to see if the biomedical system can be used to transmit medical telemetry without interfering with the voice comms.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: On Down Voice Backup S-band, how do you read me?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Actually, a good deal of that little power pack heats up and [Garble]
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Fred sounds pleased that the signal conditioner on his biomedical harness is warming up once they switched it on.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, Jack just [garble] himself back down.

And after he put on the 250 Bat A, Bat B breakers, he read Bat bus volts as 32.3 on A, 37.0 on B; he then punched in the 275 breakers, which tied the batteries to the buses, and at that time he read the A bus volts 32.0 on A, zero amps; 37.0 on B, zero amps.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: The comm got noisy there, and I want to make sure we get these numbers right, so I'd like to ask you to repeat them [garble] [Long pause.]
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: How do you read now, Joe?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Read it nice and slow so we'll be sure to get it.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Jack pushed in the 250 Bat A, Bat B entry breakers.
He read battery bus volts as 32.3 on A, 37.0 on B.
Then he pushed in the 275 breakers tying the BATs to the main buses.
In this configuration, Main A is at 32.0 volts, zero amps; Main B is at 37.0 volts, zero amps.
So it looks like the buses are okay.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: The procedure has proven that all the DC systems have a proper voltage on them.
Zero amps means that there is presently no current flowing through the system - nothing is drawing electric power from the batteries, hence the amperage is zero.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Copy those numbers and thank you very much.
I assume you got them off all right.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: We got all the breakers pulled again.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: How did our [garble] configurations appear to be working down there?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I'm sorry, Fred, but we got noisy again there.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Let me know when you want me to try it.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I'd just like to [garble] how's - how's our lith-o cartridge setup [garble] appear to be working down there.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: We are reading 0.2 on our CO2sets here, and we're all delighted.
It seems to be working fine.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: In a little while here, I'm going to have a procedure that I want to read up to you and have you copy down for future use.

It's a procedure for powering the Command Module main bus off the LM, and it's something that we feel that's going to come in real handy later on for such things as topping off the Command Module entry batteries, and also possibly for doing some preheating and preliminary powering up of the Command Module before we get rid of the LM.
We'll have that for you in probably 10 or 15 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Our procedure-generating mill has generated another very short one for you here.
It's a procedure for getting an onboard read-out ...
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: This is a procedure for getting a read-out of the descent propellant tank temps.
It's no big problem, but our LM people say that the bottom of the descent stage is probably cooling off, and we just want to verify that the descent water tank will be okay.
Right now, it looks as though it won't freeze until several hours after it's empty, but we want to have you read these temperatures out to us so we can see how good our predictions are.
The procedure is, on panel 16, close the Propellant Display/Engine Override Logic circuit breaker.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Mission Control is concerned that the primary water tank located in the Descent Stage could be dropping in temperature, potentially posing a risk of freezing while the spacecraft cools down.
Losing the descent stage water would force them to rely on the much smaller supply in the Ascent Stage and potentially run out of cooling water.
The water tank does not have a temperature sensor, hence the crew and Mission Control will check the propellant tank temperatures to get an idea of the state of the Descent Stage.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The Propellant Display Override circuit breakers are closed.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: The next step is simply to turn the Propellant Temp Press Monitor switch to Descent 1, read the fuel and Ox temps, turn it to Descent 2, read the fuel and Ox temps; tell us what they are.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: On Descent 1, I get a fuel temp of 66.5 degrees, Ox [garble] on Descent 2, I get [garble] [Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: The tank temperature procedure simply involves powering the display and then reading the details up from the displays on the LM console.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We're in noisy comm here; I got Descent 1 fuel 66.5; stand by a second.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: You got 66.5 on Descent 1 fuel temp, oxidizer temp is 66 degrees.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: On Descent TANK 2, fuel is reading 60 - Okay.
Descent 2 fuel is reading 68 degrees; oxidizer, 65 degrees.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: The fuel temperature is 20°C, the oxidizer temperature 18.3°C.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We'd like you to pull the circuit breaker, and we'll probably come to you again in 5 or 6 hours for another check.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: The panel 16 [garble] [garble] Engine [garble] Override is open.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: This in-mission photograph shows the displays powered off.
Several warning lights are illuminated on the top of the console.
Notice the sticky tape around the 8-ball indicator - they worked as quick references to help the crew figure out how to fly the awkward machine that was the docked CSM-LM.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 94 hours, 41 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
Position and velocity now showing 178,274 nautical miles [330,163 km] out from Earth.
Spacecraft weight still hovering around 87,942 pounds [39,890 kg].
94 hours, 42 minutes Ground Elapsed Time and standing by; this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're chasing a small glitch that we saw a while ago in the O2flow rate which is now normal again, but what we'd like you to do is, first of all, to tell us whether, during that canister procedure, you moved O2Demand Reg A to any position other than Cabin, and then we'd like you to move it to Off momentarily and back to Cabin for us.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: To answer your question, Joe, it's no.
I checked the Cabin all the time.
Qualitatively, when we switched to this configuration, it didn't seem like the frequency or the suit fan noise [garble] decreased [garble] logged down a little bit, but I'll follow your procedure.
You want me to take Reg A, go to close and back to Cabin; is that correct?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I'll get back to you if we see anything.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: The microphone has caught a brief snippet of the LM's namesake song being played onboard.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hey, have you guys got a woman on board?
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: No way I could handle that.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: And, Joe, do you have the [garble] out for the [Garble]?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I'm not sure I copied that, Fred.
If you asked me whether I had that powerup procedure, I don't have it for you, yet.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: The question was, do you have a [garble] from the Moon burn.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: We'll be able to get better comm.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: You want to try it, now?
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Two things: first of all, we noticed a [Garble]
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Just as you started talking, it got noisy again, Fred; try it again.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Try it now, loud and slow.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Jack had a question on how far out from the Moon are we now?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: From the Moon we are now?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Our little plot shows you just touching the 180-thousand-mile line.
So you're about 40 K out from the Moon.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: That translates to 74,000 kilometers away from the Moon.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And the other thing is, we've noticed some fresh new particles floating around outside, so possibly the Service Module is starting to vent a little bit again.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: On the O2flow thing, we clearly saw the Demand Regulator go to Off and back to Cabin.
TELMU thinks that it's no big thing that you've seen a little change in flow due to the different resistance we've got in the circuit.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Their makeshift adapter arrangement is altering the flow of air in the LM's ECS system and hence the sensors that monitor the state of the life support system are apparently giving some odd readings every now and then.
Their other theory is evidently that the crew might be fiddling with the controls, or accidentally bump at them while moving inside the spacecraft.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Recommend you go to Off on the Biomed switch.
We never did get any Biomed and it might clear up the comm a little bit.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: That might be a little better.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: We're on the 180 - K line, right now.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I got a copy of the [garble] K [Garble]
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: You know, the mode we're going to do it in.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: It appears that Fred and Jack are gossiping about the upcoming manual burn without the intention of having Mission Control listen in.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We can't help them too much.
Actually it isn't bad [garble] but [garble] [garble] goddamned unfortunate.
In part, I get a feeling [garble] now when I get [garble] Just before [garble] goes to zero in 1 second I'm going to pull them out [Garble]
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Another burn that's never been done.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Go to the - Go to the technique for a lash and [garble] it's the burn technique.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I'm sure they got something written out, maybe [Garble]
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: All you - you don't use a very good one [garble] Just if you'll go [garble] at your attitude reference for [garble] and the g-meter backup.
Because, really, you can almost [garble] The Command Module and your odds are 60 to 40 that you're going to hit Earth blunt-end first, without any reference.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: That proves you've got a little bit above 50-percent odds that you're going to enter blunt end, and...
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: We made an entry like that in Apollo 8 in a [garble] with Houston, ol' Buzz went and turned on the Command Module heaters and left them on.
The activators, they went, "Shooo!" both tanks; we had no gas.

Just had a real slow rate [garble] We actually went out in the console and started debriefing, but left it running and watched it; and the son of a bitch did a hop-skipout, a hopout, underneath [Garble], but not much, and then we did a long hop [garble] but - The second reentry, also, it did one [garble] both times.
It lucked out twice and made a real peculiar [garble] They went to zero g [Garble]
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Fredâs first mission assignment was as Backup Lunar Module Pilot for Apollo 8 with Backup Commander Neil Armstrong and Backup Command Module Pilot Edwin 'Buzz' Aldrin.
They trained extensively for the mission that was eventually performed by the prime crew.
Fred was brought in due to the original Prime Crew member Michael Collins developing health problems, which prompted former backup CMP Jim Lovell to be reassigned to the Prime crew.
Armstrong, Aldrin and a recovered Collins would form the Apollo 11 crew while Fred was assigned to Apollo 13âs crew with Lovell and Ken Mattingly.
Lovell and Haise were backup crewmembers for Apollo 11, along with Lovell's Apollo 8 LMP Bill Anders.
Incidentally, Ken Mattingly supported Apollo 8 as a CapCom in Mission Control.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The gist of the conversation between the two men appears to be Fred assuring Jack that the Command Module is capable of making a safe reentry even without many of the more advanced systems and methods usually at their disposal.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: [Garble] pass [garble] then it'll come back down.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: It - Our wobble in this PTC has picked up where the Moon is running by pretty high in the window and the Earth pretty low; so I expect - On the side where the Moon's present, we're having a little bit of trouble with the comm [garble] breakout there in a couple of places.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: [Garble] pressure gage as far as the noise [garble] get the thing from the AFT.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I'm all set to copy that procedure for powering up the Command Module from the LM, whenever you want to give it to me.
When did you, I guess you want to put Battery A out of the Charge, huh?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Jack, we're not certain when the best time is to implement this procedure, and what we want to do at this time is read it up to you, so you'll have it and understand it and we'll - We'll be able to do it quickly when the time comes.
And I don't have it for you quite yet.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: I'll be standing by to copy it whenever you get it.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Joe, I got to switch antennas here.
I read you on the Forward antenna.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Okay, Jack, I have the procedure now to read up to you.
Before I do, I want to mention something that will be of interest to Fred.
We are looking at the SHe tank pressure rise and we expect it to blow.
We don't know exactly what time it will go, somewhere around 105 hours or perhaps later than that.
We've got plenty of blowdown capability.
We just thought you ought to be informed.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: The clock is ticking on the supercritical helium tank that gave the crew worries during the early part of the mission.
Mission Control has calculated the time when it will reach critical pressure for the tank to start venting via a component known as the burst disk.
The 'blowdown capacity' mentioned is the ability to fire the DPS engine solely using the helium pressure already in the system, without having the pressurization gas replenished from the tank.
This means that the loss of the SHe tank will not mean writing off the engine completely.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Fred, they said they're looking at the SHe tank pressure rise, and they expect it to blow somewhere around 105 hours [garble] Okay.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Are you ready for the procedure, now, Jack?
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Is it a big long one, or a short one, or a medium sized [garble] about how many pages?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: It's about - I'd say 15 to 20 steps, Jack.
Some of it is LM stuff, and the rest of it is in CSM.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: I got a fresh page here, and I'm ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Kerwin will now give Jack instructions on how to transfer LM battery power into the Command Module by reversing the current in the LM umbilicals normally used to power the LM's heaters during the translunar coast with power from the Command Module's fuel cells.

Although not originally meant for this purpose, the design of the system in both spacecraft was such that it allows for power to flow the other way around as well, from the LM batteries over to the Command Module's Main Bus B.
Such a power transfer has been at the core of LM lifeboat thinking, with the idea that the LM can supply Command Module's crucial navigation systems with power.
Although this is not the case now, some official written procedures for this have been around at least since Apollo 11, with procedures found from the Operations Handbook for the spacecraft.
This is the first practical implementation of such a procedure, however.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I'll give you the LM steps first.
They have to be done first, and the first step is on panels 11 and 16, circuit breakers Ascent ECA, ASC ECA, two to close.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Panels 11 and 16 CBs Ascent ECA to closed.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: This closes the circuit breakers that supplies power to the Ascent Stage Electrical Control Assemblies, or ECAs.
They are currently using the Descent Stage batteries and hence all the Ascent Stage power systems are switched off.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Next step: panels 11 and 16 CB Ascent ECA Control, two, to close.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: On 11 and 16 CBs Ascent ECA Controls to close.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: These breakers supply power to contactors that switch the batteries on and off the ECA power feeds.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The next step is Bat 5 and 6 Normal Feed to high; those are switches.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: This gets the Ascent Stage batteries to normal power feeds.
Unlike the Descent Stage's batteries, the Ascent Stage batteries do not have the 'low voltage tap' mode for their early operations, but are expected to be used at high voltage from the beginning.
They do, however, have the backup feed mode that will allow one battery to take over from the other, should one of the two Ascent batteries fail.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Next step: Bats 1 and 3 to Off/Reset.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: This step resets the batteries 1 and 3 in the Descent Stage and takes them off the power bus.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Next step: wait 30 minutes for preconditioning.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: That's correct, and the last LM step for now is Bats 2 and 4 to Off/Reset.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: This resets the rest of the Descent Stage batteries.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Now we'll go to the CSM, and the first step there is connect LM/CSM umbilical.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Two cables (for redundancy) run from the Command Module to the Lunar Module in the docking tunnel between the spacecraft.
Normally they are plugged in as soon as the two spacecraft dock during Transposition and Docking to supply the LM with power to its heaters.
Now they are taking the first steps to reverse that connection, to transfer power from the LM into the CM instead.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The next step on panel 5, CB LM Power-1 Main B and LM Power-2 Main B, both, closed.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: On panel 5, CB LM Power-1 Main B and 2 Main B, both, closed.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: These circuit breakers in the Command Module supply CM power into the LM umbilical from Command Module's Main Bus B.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: On panel 5, CB EPS Sensor Signal Main B to close.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: EPS Sensor Signal Main B closed on panel 5.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: This breaker governs power into the sensor that produces electrical system info to the onboard displays and the telemetry.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Next step, panel 250: CB Bat B Power Entry/Postlanding; closed.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: On panel 250: CB Bat B Power Entry/Postlanding; closed.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: This breaker connects Battery B in the Command Module into the battery bus.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Verify Main Bus voltage; then, LM Power switch on panel 2 to CSM.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: That's verify the Main Bus voltage, and switch LM Power to CSM.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: The switch activates the power connection between the two spacecraft.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Then go back to 275, CB Main B Bat Bus B to open.
Hey, Jack, that's the step I skipped for you, I'm afraid.
Before verifying Main Bus voltage, you have to, on panel 275, close CB Main B Bat Bus B.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: How are you reading on the Aft Omni.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: After putting the Battery B Power Entry/Postlanding On, then I want to push the - on panel 275 Main B to Bat Bus B in, huh?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Kerwin forgot to give the instructions in the right order.
Battery B has to be enabled in the Battery Bus as well.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Then you go to verify Main Bus voltage, LM Power to CSM, and then, on panel 275, circuit breaker Main B Bat Bus B to open.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: After verifying the Main Bus voltage connecting the LM Power to CSM I want to pull Main B Bat Bus B, or Main B to Bat Bus B, open.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Either circuit breaker will then cut the battery B power into the system.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And then on panel 250, circuit breaker Bat B Power Entry/Postlanding to open, and verify Main Bus B voltage.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Panel 250 Bat B Power Entry/Postlanding open, and then verify the Main Bus voltage.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: This turns off the Battery B, and then they can check the voltage on the display to see if they are getting power from the LM into the Main Bus.
Having pulled the CM battery off, this should now be their source of power.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The last, steps are to get LM back to descent batteries.
The first step is Bats 1, 2, 3 and 4, Hi Voltage, On, Over.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Then the next step is a LM step, Bats 1, 2, 3 and 4, Hi Voltage, On.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: This is a step they performed all the way back when starting up the LM and connecting the Descent Stage batteries to high voltage taps.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: That's correct, and the last step in LM, Bats 5 and 6 to Off.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Say again you cut out there, Joe.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: The last step in LM, Batteries 5 and 6 to Off.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: This turns off the Ascent Stage batteries.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Note that circuit-breakers, protection on that circuit limits the available current to 15 amps.
We think we can do quite a bit with it though.
And the LM/CSM umbilical will be hot and Main Bus voltage may be monitored by selecting Main B on the gage.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: The power umbilical is not designed to supply a great deal of power, hence only a current of 15 amp can be normally tolerated.
It is still a considerable amount of power - about 400 watts - and will make all the difference in the future.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And I'll be ready to implement this whenever you call it up.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: And how do you read, Joe?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: While Jack was on comm, I was looking out the docking window here, and I could see another good shower off particles coming out of the Service Module.
And the position this time appears to be on the other side.
Before, we'd always seen them out of window 1, and where I'm looking at it from here, it looks like it'd be down below window 6 somewhere in the Service Module.
Jack's going upstairs to see what it looks like up there.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: And to show you how relaxed we are about the entry, Ken is looking at - giving you guys a chance to - prior to going into entry, Prep to snap a few pictures of the Service Module.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Fred is pleased to hear that his crew comrade is working for them on Earth.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Yes, he's got all that film up there, and he doesn't want to waste it.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Oh, I thought maybe you'd want me to do a PLSS EVA to go shoot pictures or something.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: They would not be able to spare the oxygen lost by depressurizing the cabin.
They are also not equipped for a deep space EVA, let alone trained for it.
Routine EVA capacity was only added for Apollo 15 and subsequent missions for the purpose of servicing the SIM Bay in the Service Module.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You're right; we got a lot of pictures.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I guess I agree it surely would be nice to document it someway if we can, but it doesn't look like [Garble].
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Yes, we think it'd be a lot of help.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Joe, how do you read me?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: We're in a bad comm mode.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: The waste transfer into the condensate can works real well, Joe.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: So, I guess we can keep FIDO happy with no overboard dumps.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Yes, that brings up one more possibility, Joe.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Now that we've got all of that stowed into the bags and the condensate can, we could probably blow all of that through the PLSS [Garble], I wonder how the sublimator would work on waste water.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: It might work if we had to do it, Fred.
Right now we're looking at a comfortable excess of water through the sublimator.

We were talking among ourselves this morning about having you try out the PLSS to ascent tank water-transfer situation and we decided not to do it, not to recommend it, because we figure it'd take us 30 hours to empty one of the ascent tanks, which you have to do in order to get PLSS water to it, and we'd rather use the descent water and we don't think we've gotten any sweat.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Fred suggests that they could load their urine into the PLSS and then pump the said waste water into the LM's water system via the Ascent Stage water tank.
Joe Kerwin is not too enthusiastic, considering that Mission Control studies have revealed that to try the water transfer, they need an empty Ascent Stage water tank.
They want to save the Ascent stage supplies as long as possible and use up the Descent Stage water first.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Haise, from1970 Technical debrief:"In fact, while we were talking about the water, and talking about feeding PLSS water into the sublimator, I had a way figured out to get the urine through the sublimator."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Lovell, from1970 Technical debrief:"Of course, at this time, we were also thinking about the PLSSs, about using the water for the sublimator and then using the fans and the batteries and the oxygen in case the LM system failed."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Swigert, from1970 Technical debrief:"It's really surprising that a lot of the things the ground sent up, we had discussed."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I don't think there's any question about it from the plumbing standpoint that we could do that if we had to.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Present thinking on the midcourse correction which would bring Apollo 13 back into the Earth's intercepting corridor, would be a LM maneuver, propulsion maneuver of about 7 feet per second at 104 hours Ground Elapsed Time and would return the spacecraft to entry corridor, shooting for a flight path angle of about minus 6½ degrees on all lunar missions to date, with a few exceptions, during the trans-Earth coast phase some sort of midcourse correction has been necessary to get back on to the corridor depending on the accuracy of the trans-Earth injection.
This particular mission is certainly no different.
Spacecraft communicator Joe Kerwin is away from the console at the moment, after having read up some additional checklist changes.
We are continuing to monitor the air-to-ground.

If the midcourse correction in this trans-Earth phase is delayed beyond 104 hours to the second one, it's in contention of second time of 118 hours, it would be somewhat greater magnitude more in the neighborhood of 40 feet per second.
With the 7-foot-per-second midcourse correction at 104 hours, the vacuum perigee would be around 20 nautical miles [37 km].
Flight path angle of minus 6.51 degrees.
At 95 hours, 32 minutes Ground Elapsed Time and standing by; this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: And, how do you read, Joe?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I think our PTC wobble is growing worse.
The Earth now is really up to the top of the window.
I have to get way down almost to the floorboards to view it.
Conversely, the Moon is way down at the bottom of the window.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Far more wobble than I noticed on my previous watch.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: And our thermal people will be looking at it.

One of the things we're talking about relative to the midcourse correction is we'd like not to do it before the SHe tank goes, if it's going to go, just so it won't give us a problem reestablishing PTC.
And right now our people think that the burst disk will pop around 106 hours.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Since you went ahead and used the PLSS condensate container, we got the procedure in for using it.

I'm not going to read it up to you except that it cautions a little bit about shaking it too much or about filling it too full, because they feel that if you do that the vent valve might unseat and the bag might leak a little bit.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: We haven't removed it from its stowage spot.
We just left it right in place, and just - he mated the tank at the end of the cable and hooked right into that.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: That's satisfactory and recommended that you leave it in the stowage spot.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: We got a - we got another flow shower going on outside.
Particles, seeing them vented against the Service Module.
Jack thinks it may be an H2vent.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: What window are you looking at it out of, Fred?
transcript.speaker: Kerwin

tokens: Somebody just handed me your latest consumables status report, and you're using between 11 to 12 amps an hour real steady, and it looks real good.
transcript.speaker: Kerwin
